event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers occupy the region, leaving obsidian tools and rock art at sites such as the Corinto caves.
Early agricultural villages cultivating maize, beans, and squash develop along the Pacific coast and interior volcanic valleys.
The Olmec-influenced center of Chalchuapa rises as an early ceremonial and trade hub in western El Salvador.
Monumental architecture appears at Chalchuapa, including the large El Trapiche pyramid.
The eruption of the Ilopango volcano (Tierra Blanca Joven) devastates central El Salvador and displaces populations.
The Maya farming village of Joya de Cerén is buried by the Loma Caldera eruption, preserving it as the 'Pompeii of the Americas.'
Maya centers in the west decline amid the broader Classic Maya collapse across Mesoamerica.
Nahua-speaking Pipil peoples migrate from central Mexico and establish polities, including the realm of Cuzcatlan.
The Pipil state of Cuzcatlan flourishes as the leading regional power, with Lenca peoples settled to the east.
Spanish conquistador Pedro de Alvarado invades Cuzcatlan but is repelled and wounded by fierce Pipil resistance.
Spanish forces found the first villa of San Salvador, beginning the colonization of the territory.
Renewed Spanish campaigns subdue Pipil resistance and firmly establish the town of San Salvador.
San Salvador receives the title of city under the Spanish crown.
Indigo (anil) production expands to become the colony's dominant cash crop, shaping its plantation economy for centuries.
The Intendancy of San Salvador is created within the Captaincy General of Guatemala, giving the province greater identity.
Priest Jose Matias Delgado leads the first cry for independence with an uprising in San Salvador against Spanish rule.
A second independence revolt erupts in San Salvador but is again suppressed by colonial authorities.
El Salvador joins the rest of Central America in declaring independence from Spain on September 15.
El Salvador joins the United Provinces of Central America after rejecting annexation by Mexico's First Empire.
El Salvador declares itself a fully sovereign republic as the Central American federation disintegrates.
Salvadoran troops join other Central American states to defeat the American filibuster William Walker.
Liberal reforms abolish communal indigenous landholdings (ejidos), accelerating the rise of a coffee-based oligarchy.
Coffee overtakes indigo as the leading export, cementing El Salvador's dependence on the coffee economy.
A short-lived Greater Republic of Central America union with Honduras and Nicaragua collapses.
President Manuel Enrique Araujo is assassinated, opening an era of rule by the coffee-planting Melendez-Quinonez dynasty.
General Maximiliano Hernandez Martinez seizes power in a coup, beginning decades of military-dominated government.
A peasant and indigenous uprising led by Farabundo Marti is crushed in 'La Matanza,' a massacre killing tens of thousands.
El Salvador becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
A reformist military coup ousts the old guard and ushers in modernizing governments and import-substitution industrialization.
The brief 'Football War' with Honduras erupts over migration and border tensions, displacing thousands of Salvadorans.
Reformist candidate Jose Napoleon Duarte is denied an apparent electoral victory through fraud, deepening polarization.
Archbishop Oscar Romero is assassinated at Mass and the FMLN guerrilla coalition forms, plunging the country into civil war.
The El Mozote massacre sees an army battalion kill around a thousand civilians, one of the war's worst atrocities.
Jose Napoleon Duarte is elected president, the first elected civilian leader amid the ongoing conflict.
FMLN guerrillas launch a major offensive on the capital, and soldiers murder six Jesuit priests at the UCA university.
The Chapultepec Peace Accords end the twelve-year civil war that killed some 75,000 people and demobilize the guerrillas into a political party.
A UN Truth Commission documents wartime atrocities, but an amnesty law shields perpetrators from prosecution.
Two devastating earthquakes strike in January and February, killing over a thousand people and leaving many homeless.
El Salvador adopts the US dollar as legal tender, replacing the colon as the national currency.
The Central America Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR) with the United States takes effect, reshaping trade.
Former FMLN journalist Mauricio Funes wins the presidency, the first leftist to hold the office since the war.
El Salvador records one of the world's highest homicide rates as gang violence between MS-13 and Barrio 18 peaks.
Outsider Nayib Bukele wins the presidency, ending three decades of dominance by the ARENA and FMLN parties.
El Salvador becomes the first country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender.
The government declares a sweeping state of exception against gangs, mass-arresting tens of thousands and slashing the homicide rate.
Nayib Bukele wins re-election by a landslide despite constitutional questions over consecutive terms.
